name: ReactJs
scope: A BASIC proficiency level individual in React should be able to create simple functional and class components using JSX and understand how to render static content. They should manage state and props to pass data between components, handle basic events like clicks and form submissions, and use React Router for simple navigation between views. They should set up new React projects using Create React App, manage dependencies with NPM or Yarn, and utilize code editors like Visual Studio Code. Understanding fundamental React concepts such as the component-based architecture, the Virtual DOM, and unidirectional data flow is essential. They should fetch and display data from external sources using the fetch API or Axios and perform basic debugging with console.log and browser developer tools. Possessing basic JavaScript ES6+ skills (like let/const, arrow functions, and array methods), along with fundamental HTML and CSS knowledge for styling components, is required. They should organize components logically, understand component reusability, and participate in team collaboration through basic Git operations and code reviews. Awareness of simple testing practices using tools like Jest, basic accessibility principles, and responsive design is also expected, along with a willingness to learn from more experienced team members.

name: Tailwind CSS
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Tailwind CSS as a competency should be able to set up Tailwind in a project, configure content paths, and integrate with build tools or frameworks; confidently use utility classes for layout, spacing, sizing, flexbox, grid, typography, color, backgrounds, borders, effects, transforms, transitions, and interactivity; apply responsive design using breakpoint prefixes and adjust layouts, spacing, and typography for different screen sizes; use state and pseudo-class variants (hover, focus, active, disabled, group, peer) to style interactive elements; compose and reuse utility patterns for common UI components, and use the @apply directive in limited cases; align utility usage with configured design tokens, support dark mode via variants and configuration, and maintain adequate contrast and accessibility; use screen-reader and focus utilities for accessible styling, and ensure interactive elements have sufficient hit area and visible states; keep class lists concise, avoid conflicts or duplication, and follow team conventions for utility order and extracted styles; debug missing or overridden styles, responsive issues, and dark mode problems using browser devtools, build logs, and configuration checks; use editor extensions for class autocompletion, linting, and sorting, and participate in code reviews, documentation, and collaboration with designers and team members; make minor configuration changes, enable plugins, and keep up to date with Tailwind documentation and release notes; and recognize when to escalate issues or seek guidance for advanced configuration or design alignment.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Tailwind Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understand and apply the utility‑first styling approach as the primary way to style user interfaces
- Use Tailwind utility classes confidently as the main mechanism for layout, spacing, typography, color, borders, and effects instead of writing ad‑hoc CSS rules
- Recognize and use Tailwind’s naming conventions for utilities (category, property, scale, variants) without frequent reference
- Distinguish when to rely on Tailwind utilities versus when a small amount of custom CSS is appropriate (while still preferring utilities by default)
- Read and interpret existing Tailwind class strings on HTML or component elements and explain what visual result they produce
- Combine multiple utilities on a single element to achieve a coherent design without conflicting or redundant styles
- Understand that Tailwind generates CSS from configured content paths and that only referenced utilities are emitted
- Use classes from Tailwind’s core utility groups: layout, flexbox/grid, spacing, sizing, typography, color, backgrounds, borders, effects, transforms, transitions, and interactivity
- Apply utility classes in a consistent and organized order aligned with commonly used sorting conventions (even if enforced by tools)
- Recognize the difference between Tailwind utilities and framework‑specific styling features (e.g., React props, Vue bindings) and use each appropriately","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Layout, Box Model, and Spacing","scope_included":"- Use layout utilities to control element display behavior (block, inline, inline‑block, flex, grid, hidden, etc.)
- Apply box model utilities to manage margin, padding, and gap across elements with consistent spacing scales
- Use width and height utilities (including min/max variants) to control sizing for common layout scenarios
- Apply flexbox utilities to arrange items in rows or columns, control wrapping, justify and align items, and adjust item growth and shrink behavior
- Use basic grid utilities to define column counts, gaps, and simple responsive grid layouts
- Apply position utilities (static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky) along with inset and z‑index utilities for layered layouts
- Use overflow utilities to control scrolling and clipping for content containers
- Apply alignment utilities to center content horizontally and vertically within flex or grid containers
- Use container‑related utilities (such as a page container class and associated width constraints) to establish page layout structure
- Maintain consistent spacing patterns across a page by adhering to Tailwind’s spacing scale instead of arbitrary pixel values wherever feasible","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Typography and Color System Usage","scope_included":"- Apply text utilities for font family, font size, font weight, line height, letter spacing, and text alignment in a consistent, scale‑based manner
- Use text color utilities to apply colors from the configured Tailwind palette and maintain consistent contrast across the interface
- Use font smoothing, text decoration, text transform, whitespace, and text overflow utilities where needed for content readability
- Apply list utilities to control list style, position, and spacing
- Use heading and body text hierarchies by combining sizing, weight, and spacing utilities to create consistent typography scales
- Apply background color utilities from the configured palette to define surface and accent areas
- Use border utilities (width, style, radius, color) to create clear visual separation and affordances
- Apply opacity utilities for text, background, and borders in a controlled manner rather than relying on arbitrary RGBA values
- Use shadow and ring utilities to create depth and focus states that fit within an existing design system
- Maintain consistency by using Tailwind’s predefined typographic and color scales instead of inventing new ad‑hoc values unless explicitly required","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Responsive Design with Tailwind","scope_included":"- Understand Tailwind’s mobile‑first responsive model and how breakpoint prefixes modify utilities at specific viewport widths
- Use standard responsive prefixes (such as small, medium, large, extra‑large breakpoints) to adjust layout, spacing, typography, and visibility
- Build responsive layouts by progressively enhancing styles from the base (unprefixed) state up through larger breakpoints
- Use responsive utilities to adjust flex direction, grid configuration, alignment, gaps, and wrapping at different screen sizes
- Adjust typography (sizes, line heights, spacing) responsively to maintain readability across devices
- Use responsive visibility utilities to show, hide, or rearrange content appropriately for different viewport sizes while preserving accessibility
- Ensure that critical interactions and content remain usable and legible on narrow viewports without requiring horizontal scrolling
- Debug responsive behavior using browser devtools and by toggling responsive modes, then adjusting Tailwind utilities to correct layout issues
- Keep responsive class combinations manageable and understandable, grouping related responsive utilities logically on elements","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"State, Interaction, and Pseudo-class Variants","scope_included":"- Use state variants (such as hover, focus, active, visited, disabled) with utilities to style interactive elements appropriately
- Ensure focus states remain visible and usable by applying focus‑related utilities rather than removing outlines without replacement
- Use group and peer variants in simple scenarios to style child or sibling elements based on parent or peer state
- Apply cursor, pointer‑events, and select utilities to control basic pointer behavior and text selection
- Use transition utilities to add simple, consistent animations for hover and focus states (duration, timing function, properties)
- Apply transform utilities (scale, rotate, translate, skew) in combination with transitions for simple interaction feedback
- Use opacity and color changes in combination with state variants to convey hover and active feedback without relying solely on subtle effects
- Ensure stateful styling remains accessible for keyboard users and does not depend only on pointer hover
- Debug missing or ineffective state styles by verifying variant prefixes, order of classes, and presence of required parent group or peer classes","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Component Composition and Reuse with Utilities","scope_included":"- Compose groups of utilities into coherent, reusable visual patterns for common UI elements
- Keep class lists on elements understandable by grouping utilities logically (layout, spacing, typography, color, effects) rather than randomly
- Identify cases where repeated groups of utilities should be refactored into reusable components at the framework level (e.g., shared component files) instead of duplicating markup
- Use the @apply directive in simple, limited cases within a Tailwind layer when multiple elements require identical low‑level styles that are difficult to manage with only utilities
- Distinguish between utility composition at the markup level and extracting higher‑level abstractions (components, partials, templates) at the framework level
- Maintain consistency across similar elements by reusing the same utility combinations instead of inventing new combinations for each instance
- Avoid over‑abstraction at this level by defaulting to utility classes and only introducing extracted styles when duplication becomes hard to maintain
- Ensure that component markup remains reasonably readable even when many utilities are required, refactoring or reorganizing as needed
- Collaborate with team members to align on simple conventions for naming extracted classes (where used) so they complement Tailwind utilities rather than conflict with them","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Theming, Dark Mode, and Design System Alignment","scope_included":"- Use configured color scales, spacing scales, and typography tokens to align implementations with an existing design system
- Apply dark mode variants to adjust colors, backgrounds, borders, and shadows for dark theme support when a project requires it
- Understand Tailwind’s dark mode strategy (class‑based or media‑based) selected in the configuration and apply dark variants accordingly
- Maintain adequate contrast in both light and dark themes using palette utilities instead of arbitrary colors that reduce accessibility
- Use semantic groupings of utilities (for surface, text, accent, and feedback styles) that align with product design guidelines
- Avoid hard‑coding design choices that conflict with shared Tailwind configuration or established tokens
- Participate in incremental adjustments to align Tailwind utility usage with evolving design direction without rewriting large sections of CSS
- Use simple configuration extensions (such as adding a small number of custom colors or spacing values) when explicitly guided by more senior team members or design references
- Keep arbitrary value usage limited and consistent when existing tokens cannot satisfy a requirement, and understand their impact on configuration discoverability","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Accessibility-aware Styling with Tailwind","scope_included":"- Preserve or enhance focus outlines using focus‑related utilities instead of removing them and leaving elements without visible focus
- Use screen‑reader utilities to visually hide content while keeping it accessible to assistive technologies when necessary
- Ensure color combinations chosen with Tailwind utilities maintain sufficient contrast for text and key interface elements
- Avoid using color alone (via text or background utilities) to communicate information when additional indicators can be added
- Use spacing and layout utilities to maintain sufficient hit area for interactive elements, improving usability on touch devices
- Ensure responsive changes do not hide essential content or controls for users who rely on keyboard navigation or assistive tools
- Confirm that disabled and error states styled with utilities remain distinguishable and accessible, not only through subtle color changes
- Cooperate with more experienced team members to adjust Tailwind utility usage based on accessibility testing feedback
- Use semantics in markup combined with Tailwind utilities so that visual styling does not compromise structural accessibility","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Code Quality, Readability, and Basic Performance Considerations","scope_included":"- Keep Tailwind class lists concise and organized, avoiding unnecessary or duplicate utilities on the same element
- Prefer reuse of existing patterns and components over ad‑hoc class combinations that introduce inconsistent design
- Avoid conflicting utilities (for example, multiple width or display utilities) on the same element unless intentionally overriding
- Structure markup so that nested levels are not deeper than necessary, relying on Tailwind layout utilities instead of extra wrappers when possible
- Understand that Tailwind’s build process removes unused utilities based on content paths, and write class strings in a way that they can be statically analyzed when practical
- Use configuration content paths correctly to prevent unused CSS from being retained and to avoid removal of necessary utilities
- Limit heavy use of arbitrary values or dynamically constructed class names when a consistent scale or static class list can achieve the same effect
- Participate in simple refactors that simplify complex class strings or layout structures to improve maintainability
- Identify when a visual requirement appears misaligned with the configured design system and raise it for discussion rather than introducing ad‑hoc patterns","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Project Setup and Tailwind Configuration (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Initialize Tailwind in a new or existing project using supported setup commands and configuration files
- Configure Tailwind’s content paths so that all relevant templates, components, and pages are scanned for class usage
- Integrate Tailwind with common build tools or frameworks used by the team (such as bundlers or meta‑frameworks) using documented steps
- Include Tailwind’s base, components, and utilities layers in the project’s CSS entry file in the correct order
- Run the development build pipeline (or dev server) so that Tailwind’s Just‑in‑Time engine (if applicable) generates utilities as they are used
- Make simple edits to tailwind.config files, such as toggling dark mode strategy or extending a small portion of the theme under guidance
- Enable and configure official Tailwind plugins where required by the project, following existing patterns within the codebase
- Understand the difference between extending the default theme and overriding it, and use extension for most project customizations at this level
- Keep configuration changes minimal, well‑scoped, and aligned with conventions established by more senior team members","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Tooling, Editor Integration, and Workflow (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Use editor extensions that provide Tailwind class name autocompletion, documentation hints, and linting feedback
- Configure or follow existing Prettier and ESLint setups that include Tailwind class sorting and formatting rules
- Use class name sorting tools or plugins that reorder Tailwind utilities consistently within markup or templates
- Work effectively with framework CLIs, dev servers, and build tools to see Tailwind styling changes reflected during development
- Use browser devtools to inspect computed styles and confirm which Tailwind utilities are affecting a given element
- Identify when a class is not being generated or applied correctly by checking the final HTML, devtools, and built CSS
- Run and understand project scripts related to building, watching, or previewing Tailwind output as part of daily development workflow
- Follow project conventions for file organization (components, pages, styles entry points) so Tailwind usage stays predictable and discoverable
- Participate in simple automation or tooling improvements related to Tailwind (such as updating sorting rules or extension settings) when guided by others","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Debugging and Troubleshooting Tailwind Issues (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Diagnose missing styles caused by incorrect class names, typos, or unsupported combinations of utilities and variants
- Identify when a utility is being overridden by another utility on the same element or a more specific rule, and adjust class order or usage accordingly
- Debug responsive issues by confirming which breakpoint variants are active in the current viewport and adjusting prefixes or utilities as necessary
- Troubleshoot dark mode issues by checking the dark mode strategy, parent class presence, and variant usage
- Resolve problems where Tailwind is not generating expected classes by examining content paths, safelists, and build logs
- Use devtools to confirm whether a style is coming from Tailwind, custom CSS, or a third‑party stylesheet and act appropriately
- Address layout problems by iteratively adjusting flex, grid, spacing, and sizing utilities and verifying the effect in the browser
- Collaborate with more experienced team members when encountering complex configuration or plugin‑related problems, providing clear descriptions and examples
- Document recurring issues and their resolutions in team knowledge bases to reduce repeated troubleshooting effort","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Code Review, and Documentation Practices (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Read and understand teammates’ Tailwind‑styled components and pages, asking clarifying questions when patterns are unclear
- Adhere to team conventions for Tailwind usage, including preferred utility order, responsive naming practices, and handling of extracted styles
- Participate in code reviews by checking for consistency of utility usage, adherence to design tokens, and avoidance of unnecessary custom CSS
- Explain the intent of Tailwind class combinations in one’s own code to reviewers, including layout decisions and responsive behavior
- Update or add simple project documentation describing how Tailwind is used in the codebase, including any basic patterns or conventions
- Coordinate with designers to confirm that utility usage aligns with design specifications and to clarify ambiguous styling requirements
- Communicate limitations or concerns when Tailwind utilities make a requirement difficult to implement cleanly, and seek agreed‑upon patterns
- Follow established branching, commit message, and pull request guidelines when making Tailwind‑related changes
- Contribute small improvements to shared components or style patterns when repeated needs are observed, after aligning with team members","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Continuous Learning and Keeping Up-to-date (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Use the official Tailwind documentation as the primary reference for utilities, variants, configuration options, and plugins
- Track major Tailwind release notes relevant to the project and understand at a high level how new features or deprecations might affect daily work
- Experiment in small, low‑risk contexts with new utilities or patterns to understand their behavior before using them widely in the codebase
- Seek feedback from more experienced colleagues on Tailwind usage patterns, refactoring opportunities, and design alignment
- Maintain a personal catalogue of frequently used utility combinations and patterns that are approved within the team context
- Adjust personal habits as team Tailwind standards evolve, such as adopting new sorting conventions, plugin usage, or configuration changes
- Recognize when a requirement goes beyond current Tailwind knowledge and proactively research recommended approaches before implementing
- Participate in internal discussions or sessions focused on Tailwind best practices and bring back applicable insights to daily work
- Reflect on past Tailwind implementation challenges and update one’s mental model of layout, responsiveness, and state styling accordingly","is_non_core_section":true}]}
